title: Tune Workflow Processes
description: Monitor and tune workflow performance using background queues, pool queues, queue pooling, and dedicated queues. Troubleshoot workflow queue entries.

topics.title: Background Queues and Queue Management

content.text: In computer science, a queue is a collection of entities stored to be processed later, following a first-in, first-out (FIFO) order. Windchill relies heavily on background queues to process many types of tasks - including email notifications, life cycle transitions, workflow activities, and indexing entries. These queues are created automatically during Windchill server startup and are configured through the wt.properties file.
Why Queue Tuning Matters

Tuning is the process of adjusting the server to improve response time and maximize throughput. Occasionally, workflow processes hang or fail to complete, and understanding how queues operate is essential for diagnosing these issues. The Queue Management utility provides a centralized view of all background queues and their current status, making it the primary tool for monitoring and troubleshooting queue behavior.

The Queue Management utility is accessed from Site > Utilities. It displays a table of all queues with columns for Name, Type, Status, Enabled/Disabled, Group, Total Entries, Waiting Entries, and Severe/Failed Entries.
Queue Management Actions

rows: Starts a stopped queue so it begins processing entries
Stops a running queue - entries remain but are not processed
Enables a disabled queue so new entries can be added
Prevents new entries from being added to the queue
Creates a new custom queue for specialized processing

content.text: Failed entries in a queue indicate that something went wrong during processing. When you see failed entries, analyze the method server log to determine the root cause before restarting or clearing the queue.

content.prompt: Match each Queue Management column to what it tells you:

pairs.right: Whether the queue is currently running or stopped
Number of entries queued but not yet processed
Number of entries that encountered processing errors
Cumulative count of all entries added to the queue

keyTakeaways: Windchill background queues process tasks such as email notifications, life cycle transitions, workflows, and indexing in FIFO order
The Queue Management utility under Site > Utilities provides a centralized view of all queues and their status, including failed entries
Queue tuning adjusts server parameters to improve response time and maximize throughput
Failed entries should always be investigated via the method server log before taking corrective action

topics.title: Workflow Queues and Pool Queues

content.text: Windchill uses three specialized background queues to manage different aspects of workflow execution. Understanding what each queue handles is essential for diagnosing why activities may not execute properly and for tuning workflow performance. All three workflow queues are pool queues by default, meaning they leverage multiple worker threads for parallel processing.
The Three Workflow Queues

rows: State change propagation for connectors, assigned activities, and links
Routing expressions, transition expressions, connector state updates
Workflow robot instantiation and execution of robot actions
Timed events, deadline checks, and schedule-based processing

content.text: When workflow activities fail to execute properly, the first step is to examine these three queues in Queue Management. Failed entries in any of them point to the specific stage of workflow processing that broke down. Periodically clean old entries to keep the queues manageable.
How Pool Queues Work

A pool queue has one main thread that creates multiple worker threads, each of which processes one queue entry at a time. All worker threads exist in the same JVM as the main thread. By default, the wt.queue.execEntriesCount property sets the thread count to 6. You can also control individual queue thread pools using wt.queue.PoolQueue.<queue_name>.ThreadPoolSize=N. Idle worker threads (daemon threads) are automatically removed after wt.queue.PoolQueue.threadKeepAlive seconds (default: 5).

Multiple workflows can execute simultaneously in the same queue. For example, if Workflow 1 and Workflow 2 both have expression robots, both robots join the WfUserWorkQueue and execute in parallel using separate worker threads - no additional configuration required.

content.prompt: Arrange the pool queue processing steps in the correct order:
items: Main thread starts and monitors the queue for new entries
Worker (daemon) threads are created as entries arrive
Each worker thread picks up and processes one entry at a time
Idle worker threads are removed after the keep-alive timeout

keyTakeaways: WfPropagationQueue handles state changes, WfUserWorkQueue handles robot execution, and WfScheduleQueue handles timed events and deadlines
All workflow queues are pool queues by default, using one main thread and multiple worker threads for parallel processing
The default thread pool size is 6, controlled by wt.queue.execEntriesCount or per-queue ThreadPoolSize properties
Multiple workflows can execute simultaneously in the same queue without additional configuration

topics.title: Queue Pooling and Dedicated Queues

content.text: While pool queues provide parallel processing through worker threads, Windchill offers two additional mechanisms for scaling workflow performance: queue pooling and dedicated queues. Queue pooling creates multiple instances of a queue for load distribution, while dedicated queues isolate specific workflow templates from the shared queue infrastructure.
Queue Pooling

Queue pooling creates a pool of multiple WfUserWorkQueue and WfPropagationQueue instances. Jobs are distributed across these queues in a round-robin (circular) manner. Each queue in the pool still produces its own sub-threads as a pool queue, so queue pooling adds a second layer of parallelism. Configure it using wt.workflow.engine.userWorkPoolSize=N and wt.workflow.engine.propagationPoolSize=N.

You do not need queue pooling just for parallel processing - pool queues already enable that by default. Use queue pooling for fault tolerance (if one queue fails, others continue) or multi-server load balancing (running queues on different background method servers).
Dedicated Queues

Dedicated queues are tied to specific workflow templates, which is helpful for frequently used or complex templates that produce long-running entries. When a template is tagged for a dedicated queue, its workflow nodes run only in that dedicated queue - keeping the shared queues free from heavy processing loads. Non-tagged workflows continue using shared queues unaffected.
Configuring Dedicated Queues

rows: Controls which queue types support dedicated queues
Globally tags all newly created templates for dedicated queues
Creates a new dedicated queue per workflow instance
Tags an individual template for dedicated queue processing

content.text: After changing the dedicatedQueueMode property, you must restart method servers for the change to take effect. To tag an individual template, check it out, open Properties > Properties tab, select the "Set dedicated queue" checkbox, then check it back in.

content.prompt: Match each scaling mechanism to when you should use it:

pairs.right: Parallel processing of entries within a single queue
Fault tolerance or distributing load across multiple servers
Isolating a heavy or frequently used workflow template
Creating separate queue instances for each workflow run

keyTakeaways: Queue pooling creates multiple queue instances with round-robin distribution - use it for fault tolerance or multi-server load balancing, not just for parallelism
Dedicated queues isolate specific workflow templates so their processing does not affect shared queues
The dedicatedQueueMode property must be set and method servers restarted before dedicated queues can be used
Individual templates are tagged for dedicated queues via the Properties tab checkbox; a global property can tag all new templates automatically

topics.title: Exercise 5 - Identify Workflow Queue Entries

content.text: In this exercise, you will initiate workflow processes and observe how their jobs appear in the workflow queues. By examining the Queue Management utility, you will see how expression robots create entries in the WfUserWorkQueue while propagation entries are processed almost immediately.

You must be signed in as wcadmin/wcadmin. The Long Running Workflow template must be available in Workflow Template Administration. This template contains an expression robot with Java code that pauses execution for 30 minutes, giving you time to observe queue entries.

content.title: Identify Workflow Queue Entries
content.objective: Initiate two workflow processes and observe the resulting entries in the WfPropagationQueue and WfUserWorkQueue.

steps.action: Sign in to Windchill as wcadmin with password wcadmin.
steps.detail: The wcadmin account has full administrative access to site utilities and workflow template administration.

steps.action: Browse to Site > Utilities > Workflow Template Administration.
steps.detail: This utility lists all workflow templates available at the site level, including the Long Running Workflow template you will use.

steps.action: Right-click Long Running Workflow and select View in Workflow Definer.
steps.detail: The Workflow Definer displays the template's graphical structure so you can inspect the expression robot and understand why the workflow runs for an extended period.

steps.action: Select the Long Running Expression robot and view its Expression tab to see the Java code that pauses execution for 30 minutes.
steps.detail: This robot uses a Thread.sleep call to keep the workflow active in the queue long enough for you to observe the entry. In a real scenario, long-running entries might indicate a complex computation or an integration call.

steps.action: Close the Workflow Definer windows.
steps.detail: You have confirmed the template structure and are now ready to initiate processes.

steps.action: Initiate the Long Running Workflow process twice, naming them Workflow-1 and Workflow-2.
steps.detail: Creating two instances lets you verify that both expression robots appear simultaneously in the queue, demonstrating parallel processing.
steps.hint: Use the Initiate action from the workflow template context menu.

steps.action: Browse to Site > Utilities > Queue Management.
steps.detail: The Queue Management utility displays all background queues and their current entries.

steps.action: View the WfPropagationQueue and confirm it has no waiting entries.
steps.detail: Propagation entries process very quickly (state changes and routing expressions), so they are typically completed before you can observe them.

steps.action: View the WfUserWorkQueue and confirm it shows two entries - one for each workflow's expression robot.
steps.detail: Both expression robots from Workflow-1 and Workflow-2 are executing simultaneously in the same queue, each occupying a separate worker thread.
steps.hint: If you see fewer than two entries, the expression robot from one workflow may have completed. Re-initiate as needed.

steps.action: Close the browser.
steps.detail: You have confirmed that expression robots create long-lived entries in the WfUserWorkQueue while propagation entries process almost instantly.

keyTakeaways: Expression robots create entries in the WfUserWorkQueue that persist for the duration of robot execution
WfPropagationQueue entries process very quickly and are rarely visible in Queue Management
Multiple workflow instances execute simultaneously in the same queue using separate worker threads

topics.title: Exercise 6 - Configure Queue Pooling

content.text: In this exercise, you will configure queue pooling to create two WfUserWorkQueue instances and verify that workflow processes are distributed across them. This demonstrates how queue pooling uses round-robin distribution to balance load.
This exercise requires stopping and restarting Windchill, which takes approximately 5 minutes. Ensure no other users are actively working on the system before proceeding.

content.title: Configure Queue Pooling
content.objective: Set the userWorkPoolSize property to 2, restart Windchill, initiate two workflows, and verify that each process is assigned to a different shared queue.

steps.action: Open the Windchill Shell.
steps.detail: The Windchill Shell provides command-line access to Windchill utilities including xconfmanager for property management.

steps.action: Stop Windchill by running: windchill stop
steps.detail: Windchill must be stopped before modifying wt.properties to ensure the changes are applied cleanly on startup.

steps.action: Set the queue pooling property by running: xconfmanager -t codebase/wt.properties -s wt.workflow.engine.userWorkPoolSize=2 -p
steps.detail: This creates two WfUserWorkQueue instances (WfSharedUserWorkQueue0 and WfSharedUserWorkQueue1) that will share the workflow robot processing load.

steps.action: Start Windchill by running: windchill start - then wait approximately 5 minutes for full startup.
steps.detail: The server needs time to initialize all services and create the new queue instances. Queue Management will not show the new queues until startup is complete.
steps.hint: Monitor the method server log to confirm startup is complete.

steps.action: Sign in to Windchill as wcadmin with password wcadmin.
steps.detail: You need administrative access to initiate workflow processes.

steps.action: Initiate the Long Running Workflow twice, naming the instances Process-1 and Process-2.
steps.detail: Two processes are needed to verify round-robin distribution across the pooled queues.

steps.action: Browse to Site > Utilities > Queue Management.
steps.detail: You should now see two shared UserWork queues instead of the single WfUserWorkQueue.

steps.action: View WfSharedUserWorkQueue0 and confirm it contains the expression robot entry from Process-1.
steps.detail: The first process initiated is assigned to the first queue in the round-robin sequence.

steps.action: View WfSharedUserWorkQueue1 and confirm it contains the expression robot entry from Process-2.
steps.detail: The second process is assigned to the next queue, demonstrating the circular distribution pattern. Each queue processes its entries independently.
steps.hint: If both entries appear in the same queue, verify that the userWorkPoolSize property was set correctly and the server was fully restarted.

keyTakeaways: Queue pooling is configured by setting wt.workflow.engine.userWorkPoolSize in wt.properties and requires a full server restart
Pooled queues are named WfSharedUserWorkQueue0, WfSharedUserWorkQueue1, etc.
Workflow processes are distributed across pooled queues in a round-robin (circular) manner

topics.title: Exercise 7 - Configure Dedicated Queues

content.text: In this exercise, you will enable dedicated queue mode for UserWork queues, tag a specific workflow template for dedicated queue processing, and verify that a new dedicated queue is created when the tagged template runs.

You must be signed in as wcadmin/wcadmin. The Long Running Workflow 2 template must be available in Workflow Template Administration. After modifying the dedicated queue property, you do not need to restart Windchill for this exercise - the template tagging step handles queue creation at process initiation time.

content.title: Configure Dedicated Queues
content.objective: Enable dedicated queue mode, tag the Long Running Workflow 2 template, initiate two processes, and verify that a new dedicated queue is created for the tagged template.

steps.action: Open the Windchill Shell.
steps.detail: You will use xconfmanager to set the dedicated queue mode property.

steps.action: Set the dedicated queue mode by running: xconfmanager -t codebase/wt.properties -s wt.workflow.engine.dedicatedQueueMode=userWork -p
steps.detail: This enables dedicated queue support for UserWork queues. Templates that are tagged will get their own queue instead of using the shared WfUserWorkQueue.

steps.action: Close the Windchill Shell.
steps.detail: The property is now set. The next step is to tag a specific template.

steps.action: As wcadmin, browse to Site > Utilities > Workflow Template Administration.
steps.detail: You need to locate the Long Running Workflow 2 template and modify its properties.

steps.action: Right-click Long Running Workflow 2 and select Edit to check it out.
steps.detail: The template must be checked out before you can modify its properties.

steps.action: Open Properties > Properties tab and select the "Set dedicated queue" checkbox.
steps.detail: This tags the template so that any workflow process created from it will use a dedicated queue rather than the shared WfUserWorkQueue.

steps.action: Save the changes, close the properties dialog, and check in the template.
steps.detail: Checking in the template makes the dedicated queue tag active for all future process instances.

steps.action: Initiate the Long Running Workflow 2 template twice, naming the instances Dedicated Process-1 and Dedicated Process-2.
steps.detail: Both processes will use the tagged template's dedicated queue, keeping the shared queue free from their long-running expression robots.

steps.action: Browse to Site > Utilities > Queue Management.
steps.detail: You should see a new WfUserWorkQueue with a 6-digit identifier - this is the dedicated queue created for the tagged template.

steps.action: Open the new dedicated WfUserWorkQueue and confirm that both expression robots (from Dedicated Process-1 and Dedicated Process-2) are executing in it.
steps.detail: Both processes from the tagged template share the same dedicated queue, which is separate from the shared WfUserWorkQueue used by non-tagged templates.
steps.hint: If you do not see the new queue, verify that the dedicatedQueueMode property was set to 'userWork' and the template's "Set dedicated queue" checkbox is enabled.

keyTakeaways: Dedicated queue mode is enabled by setting wt.workflow.engine.dedicatedQueueMode in wt.properties
Individual templates are tagged for dedicated queues by selecting the "Set dedicated queue" checkbox in the template's Properties tab
A dedicated queue appears in Queue Management with a unique 6-digit identifier, separate from the shared queues
All processes from a tagged template share the same dedicated queue, isolating their workload from non-tagged workflows
